topic: Kernel's annual vet visit — she's healthy

turns.content: kernel survived her annual vet visit and i survived kernel surviving her annual vet visit
Sounds like it was eventful for both of you. How did it go?

so the good news is she's completely healthy. weight is good, teeth are fine, no issues. the bad news is the process of determining this involved her escaping the carrier twice, scratching the vet tech, and producing a sound i can only describe as a tiny demon being exorcised

The tiny demon exorcism sound is a classic cat-at-vet vocalization. At least the medical outcome is good — healthy teeth especially is a win since dental issues are one of the most common (and expensive) cat health problems.

Did the vet tech survive the scratching?

she was a professional about it. said "oh she's feisty" which is vet tech code for "your cat is a menace." but honestly kernel is only like this at the vet. at home she's the most chill animal alive. she literally sleeps on my keyboard while i code and doesn't flinch when i type. but put her in a carrier and she becomes a different creature

Carrier-triggered personality changes are universal in cats. It's like they have a firmware switch: home mode vs. transport mode, and transport mode activates all threat responses simultaneously.

Did you try any calming tricks this time, or was it just the standard grab-and-stuff approach?

i tried the thing where you leave the carrier open in the apartment for a week beforehand so she gets used to it. she went in there exactly once, on day 2, peed in it, and never went back. so i had to wash the carrier and then physically insert her this morning which involved a towel, some treats, and a lot of negotiation

The strategic carrier pee is a power move. She made her feelings about the carrier perfectly clear and then forced you to deal with the logistics. Cats are masters of nonverbal communication.

The towel burrito method is usually the most reliable approach for carrier-resistant cats. Did the treats help at all or did she see through the deception?

she ate the treats AND resisted the carrier. got the benefit without any of the compliance. honestly i respect it. she's optimizing for maximum reward with minimum cooperation. there's a game theory paper in there somewhere

Kernel has achieved the Nash equilibrium of treat negotiation: consume all offered incentives, concede nothing. She would be a terrifying contract negotiator.

So she's healthy, she's sharp, and she's still running the household. Any recommendations from the vet or was it a clean bill of health all around?

clean bill. vet said she's in great shape for her age, keep doing what i'm doing. which is basically let her eat, sleep on warm electronics, and occasionally chase a laser pointer at 11pm when i'm procrastinating on code reviews. the vet also said i should brush her teeth which, lol. i would like to keep my fingers

Cat tooth brushing is one of those things vets recommend and approximately 3% of cat owners actually do. The other 97% value their fingers. Dental treats or water additives are a less dangerous compromise if you want to do something without risking injury.

Sounds like Kernel is thriving though. Good to have the annual confirmation that the tiny keyboard-sleeping overlord is in good health.

genau. she's currently passed out on the couch recovering from the trauma of being looked at by a stranger. i should also recover. the whole trip took 90 minutes and i'm more exhausted than after a 4-hour debugging session. next year i'm considering a vet that does house calls just to skip the carrier war

House call vets are genuinely great for cats who hate carriers — the cat stays in their territory and is usually much calmer. It's a bit more expensive but the savings in emotional damage (yours and Kernel's) might be worth it. Look into it before next year so you have someone lined up.

yeah the cost is whatever. i once spent 80 euros on a rubber duck debugging session that lasted three hours and produced zero results. paying a bit more for a vet to come here so kernel doesn't lose her mind and i don't get a cardio workout wrestling her into a box seems like a better use of money. plus she'd probably actually let the vet examine her properly if she's on her own couch instead of in full fight-or-flight on a metal table

Cats are absolutely calmer in their own environment — the metal table plus unfamiliar smells is what triggers most of the panic. A home visit would probably mean a faster, more thorough exam with less drama. And you could skip the entire carrier negotiation arc, which sounds like it would save you significant emotional bandwidth. Enjoy the post-vet recovery — you've both earned it.
